description: Hand-curated Widespread Panic song origins written in Burnthday's own voice, facts extracted from fan newsletters (Moon Times / The Panicle), band interviews, and the web, then paraphrased into original prose with sources cited. This is facts-into-original-prose, NOT republication. It is a supplement to the Facebook-sourced song-origins.json: entries with mode 'new' are songs not yet in that set; mode 'augment' entries add or correct facts on an existing origin without duplicating what is already there. The build merges these by slug.

conventions.mode: 'new' = a song not in song-origins.json; 'augment' = adds/corrects facts on an existing entry
conventions.sources: Every factual claim is traceable to a listed source. Unverifiable claims are omitted (evidence discipline).
conventions.attribution: Moon Times = official WSP newsletter (Internet Archive); The Panicle = fan transcription (widespread-panic.blogspot.com); interviews credited to outlet + date.

origins.title: North
origins.summary: “North” is a song by Jerry Joseph, covered by Widespread Panic. It appears on Dirty Side Down (2010).

quotes.text: The song was written by Jerry Joseph a long time ago, probably when we first met him, around 16 years ago.
quotes.speaker: John Bell

quotes.text: A lot of that I wrote in Nicaragua... I still had this kinda idealic vision of the Northwest. [laughing] Burns out really quickly. As soon as you're there for three days in the rain you're like, ‘what the fuck was I thinking.’
quotes.speaker: Jerry Joseph

origins.notes: A Jerry Joseph cover; one of two covers on Dirty Side Down (2010). Warren Haynes sat in on it at Wanee, 2010.

sources.label: Jerry Joseph, “Passion and Pain” interview (Part I)
John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

clusters.label: Covers and where they came from

faq.q: Who wrote “North”?
faq.a: “North” was written by Jerry Joseph, and is covered by Widespread Panic.

faq.q: Is “North” a Widespread Panic original or a cover?
faq.a: It is a cover of a song by Jerry Joseph.

faq.q: What album is “North” on?
faq.a: It appears on Dirty Side Down (2010).

faq.q: When did Widespread Panic first play “North”?
faq.a: Its live debut was 9/25/99.

faq.q: How many times has Widespread Panic played “North”?
faq.a: About 287 times (per Everyday Companion).

origins.title: Time Zones
origins.summary: “Time Zones” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on Earth to America (2005).

quotes.text: It's funny because when we were writing them, Jerry and I thought ‘Time Zones’ would be the big new Panic anthem and ‘Second Skin’ would be this Massive Attack-style jam, but once they were played live, they kind of flipped roles.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

quotes.text: With ‘Time Zones,’ which I thought was real hip, there were references to To Kill a Mockingbird. So I went to the library and got the Cliff Notes and the book...
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.notes: Written by Schools & Jerry Joseph in the same session as “Second Skin.” On Earth to America (2005).

sources.label: Dave Schools & John Bell, “New Frontiers” by Andy Tennille

faq.a: About 126 times (per Everyday Companion).

origins.title: Her Dance Needs No Body
origins.summary: “Her Dance Needs No Body” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: This song is more deserving of just verse chorus, verse chorus, bridge and solo and out kind of thing... the musical beginnings of ‘Three Candles’ and ‘Her Dance’... coming from Jimmy [Herring].
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.title: Raise The Roof
origins.summary: “Raise The Roof” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on Ain't Life Grand (1994).

quotes.text: The song came from Mikey's direction and we all just hauntingly followed along during the demo sessions, and cleaned up the vocals on the demos, and left it like that... There's no place really to go except maybe put some more guitars on, add a third vocal part... In essence, it had what it had, you know?
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.notes: On Ain't Life Grand (1994); mostly retired after 2001 (last on 6/22/01).

sources.label: John Bell, R&R interview, Grady's Restaurant, Atlanta

origins.title: Gomero Blanco
origins.summary: “Gomero Blanco” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: Gomero Blanco: First recorded during the May 1987 sessions at John Keene's Studio in Athens for the initial release of ‘Space Wrangler.’ While this song is not played live, several shows at the Uptown Lounge featured similar sounding jams.
quotes.speaker: The Panicle

origins.notes: A studio-era track; not a live staple.

origins.title: Bear's Gone Fishin'
origins.summary: “Bear's Gone Fishin'” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999).
origins.notes: Debuted as an instrumental in July 1997; lyrics added ~two years later. Named for the band's Colorado friend Thomas “Bear” Guenther (d. 2019), the same “Bear” behind “Postcard.” On 'Til the Medicine Takes (1999). Source: JamBase; Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech.

sources.label: Widespread Panic at Red Rocks feature (Bear Guenther, instrumental-to-lyrics timeline)
Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech

origins.title: Aunt Avis
origins.summary: “Aunt Avis” is a song by Vic Chesnutt, covered by Widespread Panic. It appears on Bombs & Butterflies (1997).

quotes.text: Vic Chestnutt deserves to win Grammy after Grammy. He's the Bob Dylan of the ’90s. We just made a video with Billy Bob. He directed the video for the song ‘Aunt Avis.’ Laura Dern is in it. Vic is in it. It was filmed in Arkansas.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

origins.notes: A Vic Chesnutt song; on Bombs & Butterflies (1997). Thornton also directed a 1991 WSP documentary.

sources.label: Debut date & Billy Bob Thornton-directed video

origins.title: Sleepy Monkey
origins.summary: “Sleepy Monkey” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on Space Baby 45 (1986).

quotes.text: We drove to Atlanta from our King Avenue house to pick up a box of 500 45's from the presser.
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.notes: The band's self-released 1986 debut 45 on their own Space Baby label (500 copies); a shortened “Monkey Image” was the B-side.

sources.label: Widespread Panic debut-single reissue (Space Baby label, 500 copies, JB's account)

origins.title: Wondering
origins.summary: “Wondering” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on Everyday (1993).

quotes.text: ‘Wondering’ is a song Mikey wrote. We never really knew what to do with it until the ‘Every Day’ sessions. That song is one of the [few] examples of a song that we just hammered and hammered and hammered in the studio. I think by the end the take that was used on the record was like the forty-eighth take... It's just one of those ‘Hey man, what are you up to?’ kind of songs!
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

origins.notes: Appears on Everyday (1993).

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Pilgrims
origins.summary: “Pilgrims” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: It's a song about riding from town to town. You're listening to a song on the radio and it fades out, and then another city's radio station fades in over top of it. It's like the same song ’cause we're travelling all the time, and the radio becomes a song in and of itself.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Makes Sense To Me
origins.summary: “Makes Sense To Me” is a song by Daniel Hutchens (Bloodkin), covered by Widespread Panic.

quotes.text: That's one that Danny Hutchins wrote, for Bloodkin, and actually after they played it he thought to himself that it sounded more like us. So he presented it to us, and we liked it, ’cause we like those guys a whole lot. And it works for us.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

origins.notes: A Bloodkin (Daniel Hutchens) song. Source: Everyday Companion.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Can't Get High
origins.summary: “Can't Get High” is a song by Daniel Hutchens (Bloodkin), covered by Widespread Panic.

quotes.text: Another great Danny song. [It's] about whatever you need to satisfy your soul, not being there. Not being able to feel feelings of elation or euphoria because whatever you need to do that is not there. I.E. a girlfriend, your pet, some sleep, whatever.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

origins.notes: A Bloodkin (Daniel Hutchens) song; the band's radio single, performed on Late Night with Conan O'Brien (1995).

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Junior
origins.summary: “Junior” is a song by Junior Kimbrough, covered by Widespread Panic.

quotes.text: ‘Junior’ is a Junior Kimbrough song. It's actually a medley of two or three Junior Kimbrough songs... plays blues, and uh, it's a great song. It's a great riff... It's just a rocket blues song.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

origins.notes: A cover of the north Mississippi hill-country bluesman Junior Kimbrough.

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Love Tractor
origins.summary: “Love Tractor” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: ‘Love Tractor,’ musically it's a lick that we used to jam around on... because it sounded so much like the Athens band Love Tractor. Who is a great band and we're all big fans... we were calling it ‘That Love Tractor Thing.’ [So] JB wrote a song about a guy who loves his tractor. Just loves riding around on his tractor!
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Dream Song
origins.summary: “Dream Song” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: ‘Dream Song’ was a lullabye that Mike wrote... to his child, Waker, when he was a baby. I'm not even sure if he wrote the words or if JB took an idea, lyrically, that Mike had, and actually wrote the words. It's a beautiful song. It's one I get to play fretless on.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Little Kin
origins.summary: “Little Kin” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: ‘Little Kin’ is a... lyrically I can't really go into it too much; it's one of JB's and it seems highly personal... I luuuuv that song!... it's kind of personal to me too because I'm an adopted child... I guess it's about childhood and learning to deal with the things of life. As far as a bass player, I love it cause it's just a slab of greasy, raw funk.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

sources.label: Dave Schools, "Rock & Road" interview at O.B. & Jack's House (4/1/95), via the Spreadnet mailing list

origins.title: Vacation
origins.summary: “Vacation” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: There was one we wanted to put on the record called ‘Vacation’ that we had never recorded before but have been playing live on and off for eight years. We did one take of it, and John Keane comes back on the monitor and says, ‘Well that was nine and a half minutes long.’ So we knew that wasn't really a possibility.
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools

origins.notes: Nearly recorded for Bombs & Butterflies (1997).

sources.label: Dave Schools, The Music Monitor #94 (Pat Lall), May 1997, via Spreadnet

origins.title: Cotton Was King
origins.summary: “Cotton Was King” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on Dirty Side Down (2010).

quotes.text: It's a tribute to New Orleans, past, present, and future. Yeah, that was fun to write... it uses the phrase ‘before the beginning,’ which I actually got from Firesign Theatre... It dates back to the beginning of time that a place like New Orleans was always meant to be created. It was inevitable, a geographic hot spot where things were going to happen no matter what, and New Orleans is what manifested.
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.notes: Closing track on Dirty Side Down (2010).

sources.label: John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

origins.title: St. Louis
origins.summary: “St. Louis” is a Widespread Panic original. It appears on Dirty Side Down (2010).

quotes.text: We've been playing that on stage as a little bit of a transition piece. It took on a little more solid shape in the studio. There's a free-flowing, open, airy jam there in the middle of it... Oh, let's just stick the ‘St. Louis’ jam in front of that [‘Shut Up and Drive’], and you've got a long intro then.
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.notes: Instrumental; on Dirty Side Down (2010).

sources.label: John Bell, "True to Our Nature" interview by Randy Ray

quotes.text: Asked whether “Shut Up and Drive” fulfills the title's potential as a great road tune: “That's what it was meant for.

origins.title: Postcard
origins.summary: “Postcard” is a Widespread Panic original.

quotes.text: When the band started we all lived in this little house in Athens, Georgia, called King Avenue, and we had a friend. His name was Tom Gunther. We commonly knew him as Bear. He left town... Some months later we got a postcard. It arrived at the band house. On the front side of it was a skier catching air way up in the mountains. On the back side was a post mark from Telluride, Colorado, and a very simple message on the postcard that said, ‘The air here is great. We're having an ass-kicking time. This town is nuts. My kind of place. I don't ever want to leave.’
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools
quotes.source: Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech

quotes.text: ...we had a buddy that wrote us a postcard and that turned into a song about 7 years ago. He gets little 15 cent royalty checks.
quotes.speaker: John Bell

origins.notes: “Bear” is Tom Guenther, the same friend behind “Bear's Gone Fishin'.”

sources.label: Dave Schools, Colorado Music Hall of Fame induction speech
John Bell & Todd Nance, KGSR interview, 4/12/97 (the royalty-check aside)

origins.title: Fishing
origins.summary: “Fishing” is a Widespread Panic original and the opening track of Ball (2003).

quotes.text: The song ‘Fishing,’ the first song on the record, started with Todd and I going in and just jamming on... bass and drums, just fooling around. We went back and made a loop out of it. John [Keane] copied the loop and started fooling around with some guitar, came up with a little flamenco riff, and JB had been sitting there in the control room on the sofa. All of a sudden he starts flipping through his notebook and says ‘Can I cut some vocals? I’ve got something that I wrote a while ago.’
quotes.speaker: Dave Schools
quotes.source: Relix, Oct/Nov 2003, "Southeast of Eden" by John Swenson

origins.notes: The opening track on Ball, and the first song written for it after Michael Houser's death.

sources.label: Relix, Oct/Nov 2003, "Southeast of Eden" by John Swenson (magazine scan)

origins.title: Papa Johnny Road
origins.summary: “Papa Johnny Road” is a Widespread Panic song written by John Hermann and John Bell, on Ball (2003).

quotes.text: JoJo and I wrote that. That was his initial inspiration. Most of the music was from JoJo’s magic brain. Then he and I did the red wine and napkin thing and came up with the lyrics.
quotes.speaker: John Bell
quotes.source: Relix, Oct/Nov 2003, "Southeast of Eden" by John Swenson

quotes.text: Is Papa Johnny Road a real place? Yeah, in Mississippi.
quotes.speaker: John Bell
quotes.source: Relix, Oct/Nov 2003, "Southeast of Eden" by John Swenson

origins.notes: Papa Johnny Road is a real place in Mississippi. On Ball (2003).

quotes.text: For ‘Nebulous,’ George and I messed with some of the words and patterns so they would fit in... George would come up with some of the notes and we got together and found the flow. He had like three pages of notes and he kind of consolidated them.
quotes.speaker: JoJo Hermann
quotes.source: Relix, Oct/Nov 2003, "Southeast of Eden" by John Swenson

origins.notes: An early George McConnell co-write. On Ball (2003).

origins.notes: One of the last songs Michael Houser showed the band before his death in 2002.

faq.q: How many times has Widespread Panic played “Counting Train Cars”?
faq.a: About 36 times (per Everyday Companion).

schema.note: Structured for SEO: each origin maps to schema.org MusicComposition (composer/lyricist, recordedAs album) with quotes[] as Quotation objects (creator=speaker, citation=source/url) and sameAs authority links. No prose narration; Burnthday is compiler, sources speak for themselves.
schema.fields: title, slug, type, isCover, performedBy, composer, originalArtist, aliases[], albums[{name,year}], firstPlayed(ISO), firstPlayedDisplay, timesPlayed, summary, quotes[{text,speaker,speakerRole,source,sourceDate,url}], notes, sources[{label,publisher,url}], sameAs[]
schema.enrichment: Each origin also carries: clusters[] (type/writer/album/theme pillar-cluster tags), related[] (sibling origins with a why), albumArt (cover asset path when a WSP release), and faq[] (pre-computed Q&A for FAQPage JSON-LD). Songs cross-link to their on-site song page (lyrics/chords/tabs/live-history) by matching title/slug.

acknowledgments.name: Ethan Ice
acknowledgments.for: providing scans of the Relix (Oct/Nov 2003) "Southeast of Eden" feature, which sourced the Ball-era song origins (Fishing, Papa Johnny Road, Nebulous, Travelin' Man, Tortured Artist, Meeting of the Waters, Counting Train Cars)
